content_en:
Radd al-Muhtar covers the complete range of Hanafi jurisprudence with a depth and comprehensiveness that has not been surpassed. In the worship chapters, it provides the most detailed systematic treatment of Hanafi ritual law available in any single work, addressing not only the standard cases but the full range of unusual and difficult scenarios that the earlier literature had addressed only partially or inadequately. The chapters on prayer and purity are models of systematic legal writing.

In commercial law, Radd al-Muhtar is particularly valuable for its treatment of the complex financial transactions of the late Ottoman period. Ibn Abidin addresses the instruments of Ottoman commercial practice — bills of exchange (suftajah), letters of credit, partnership structures used in long-distance trade — and situates them within the Hanafi legal framework. His treatment of these questions was an important contribution to the ongoing effort to apply the principles of Islamic commercial law to the evolving commercial environment of the Ottoman economy.

The family law chapters are among the most practically significant sections of the work. Ibn Abidin's treatment of marriage, divorce, custody, and inheritance is thorough and attentive to the specific disputes and ambiguities that arise in real legal cases. His discussion of the rights of women — to dower, to maintenance, to custody of children, and to protection from arbitrary divorce — reflects both the legal principles of the Hanafi school and a concern for the fairness of legal outcomes.

A distinctive feature of Radd al-Muhtar is its treatment of questions that earlier commentators had avoided or treated inadequately. Ibn Abidin regularly notes that a question is one that 'requires investigation' (ta'ammul) and then provides that investigation himself. This willingness to engage with difficult and unsettled questions, rather than simply reporting established positions, reflects the genuine legal scholarship that distinguishes Radd al-Muhtar from mere compilation.
